question: In Java, which keyword is used to resume the execution of a suspended thread?
options: resume()
notify()
start()
run()
explanation: The notify() method (or notifyAll()) is used to wake up a thread that is waiting on the object's monitor, effectively resuming its execution.

question: What is the primary purpose of the Thread.yield() method in Java?
options: To terminate the current thread
To pause the current thread indefinitely
To resume another waiting thread
To hint to the scheduler that the current thread is willing to yield its current use of the processor
explanation: Thread.yield() is a hint to the thread scheduler that the current thread is willing to yield its current use of a processor, potentially allowing other threads of the same priority to resume or run.

question: A thread is currently waiting after calling object.wait(). Which of the following is NOT a valid way to resume this thread?
options: Calling object.notify() from another thread
Calling object.notifyAll() from another thread
The thread is interrupted by another thread
Calling object.resume() from another thread
explanation: The resume() method is deprecated and related to the old Thread.suspend() method, not the wait()/notify() mechanism. It is not a valid way to resume a thread waiting on an object's monitor.

question: In the context of the Java Memory Model, what guarantee does a call to Thread.start() provide regarding variable visibility between threads?
options: It guarantees no visibility of variables
It guarantees that all variables will be synchronized
It creates a happens-before relationship, so actions before start() in the parent thread are visible to the new thread after it starts
It only guarantees visibility for final fields
explanation: Calling start() on a thread creates a happens-before relationship. Any actions taken by the thread that called start() before that call are visible to the new thread after it has begun execution.

question: Consider a scenario where multiple threads are waiting on a shared lock object. Thread A calls lock.notify(). Shortly after, Thread B calls lock.notifyAll(). Which statement is true about which waiting threads will resume?
options: Only one arbitrary thread will resume after the first notify(), then all remaining waiting threads will resume after the notifyAll()
No threads will resume because notify() and notifyAll() cancel each other out
All waiting threads will resume immediately after the first notify() call
The JVM will throw an IllegalMonitorStateException for the second call
explanation: The first notify() call will wake one arbitrarily chosen thread waiting on the lock. Later, the notifyAll() call will wake every other thread that is still waiting on the same lock at that moment. They do not cancel each other; each call has its own effect.
